Q1: Honestly, my study routine was all over the place. I couldn't afford a consistent schedule because I was dealing with so much outside of school—work, family stuff, just life being chaotic. I'd squeeze in studying whenever I could, usually late at night or in random pockets of time between everything else. It wasn't organized at all, more like survival mode where I'd cram when absolutely necessary.
Q2: My pattern was completely driven by deadlines. I didn't have the luxury of studying at regular times because my life was too unpredictable. When an assignment was due, I'd go into crisis mode and dedicate whatever hours I could find to getting it done. Some weeks I barely touched anything, and other weeks I was up until 3 AM trying to catch up on everything at once.
Q3: Usually panic about a deadline is what got me to log in. I'd suddenly remember something was due or I'd get an email notification, and that would jolt me into action. Sometimes I logged in just to check if I'd missed anything important since my attendance was so low. It was rarely a calm, planned decision—more like an emergency response.
Q4: I wouldn't say the LMS ever became a normal routine for me. There were brief periods where I tried to check in more regularly, but something would always come up and throw me off track. The closest it got to routine was when I had a string of assignments due and I was logging in every other day just to stay afloat. But that was more crisis management than habit.
Q5: The discussion posts were actually helpful because I could learn from what other students were thinking without having to attend every lecture. I also found the assignment submission area useful because it was straightforward—upload and done. Videos were okay when I could actually watch them, but I preferred things I could engage with quickly since my time was so limited.
Q6: I ignored a lot of the supplementary readings and extra resources because I simply didn't have time. If it wasn't directly tied to an assignment grade, it got pushed to the bottom of my list and usually stayed there. The longer videos I'd often skip or only watch parts of because sitting through 40 minutes felt impossible with everything else going on.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a direct connection to my grade or if it helped me understand something I was struggling with immediately. I needed to feel like the effort would pay off right away because I couldn't afford to invest time in things that might be useful someday. Practical, hands-on stuff that I could apply to assignments—that's what mattered to me.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't afford. Required ones got done because they had to, even if I was exhausted or stressed. Optional stuff, no matter how interesting it might have been, just couldn't compete with everything else demanding my attention. There was always this guilt about skipping them, but I had to prioritize survival over enrichment.
Q9: Absolutely, I did a ton of studying offline. I'd download PDFs and readings to my phone so I could study during my commute or breaks at work. I also made handwritten notes because physically writing things helps me remember better—that's just how I learn. A lot of my real studying happened away from any screen that could track it.
Q10: There were many times I'd be working through problems in a notebook or re-reading printed materials while the LMS showed nothing. I'd sometimes watch a video once, take detailed notes, and then study those notes for hours without ever logging back in. My kinesthetic learning style means I need to do things with my hands, so a lot of my engagement was physical and completely invisible online.
Q11: Yes, constantly. I'd access a lecture video or reading once, download or screenshot what I needed, and then work with that material offline for days. My internet access isn't always reliable, and honestly, I found it easier to focus on printed or downloaded materials anyway. The LMS would show one quick login, but the actual learning happened over much longer periods offline.
Q12: There were definitely times when everything felt impossible. When my anxiety was really high or I was dealing with personal crises, even opening the LMS felt overwhelming. I'd see how much I'd missed and just freeze. The mindfulness exercises they introduced helped a bit, but there were still weeks where I could barely function, let alone engage with coursework.
Q13: All of those things affected me constantly. Fatigue was huge because I was always running on too little sleep. Competing responsibilities meant school often came last after work and family obligations. When I was confused about material and couldn't attend office hours because of my schedule, I'd sometimes just avoid the whole topic until I absolutely couldn't anymore.
Q14: Oh definitely. Sometimes I'd be scrolling through materials or watching a video, but my mind was somewhere else entirely—worrying about bills, thinking about a family problem, just mentally checked out. I'd realize I'd been staring at the screen for twenty minutes and absorbed nothing. The LMS would log that time as engagement, but it was anything but.
Q15: I postponed things all the time, even when I knew it would make things worse. The anxiety would build up around certain assignments, and avoiding them felt easier in the moment than facing that stress. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow when I felt better, but tomorrow always came with its own problems. It was a cycle I couldn't seem to break.
Q16: Deadlines were everything for me. They were the only thing that could override my avoidance and anxiety. I'd calculate exactly how much time I had and then work backward, usually cutting it way too close. The LMS would show these bursts of activity right before due dates because that's when the pressure finally outweighed the fear.
Q17: When I got good feedback, it surprised me and actually motivated me to try harder on the next thing. I wasn't expecting to do well given how little I was able to attend class. But when feedback was delayed or vague, it didn't really change anything because I'd already moved on to the next crisis. Immediate, specific feedback was the only kind that really affected me.
Q18: The course structure was honestly pretty flexible, which saved me. If it had required more synchronous participation, I would have failed for sure. Being able to submit assignments and engage with materials on my own schedule was the only reason I could manage six online courses. But the flexibility also meant I had to be self-disciplined, which was hard when I was barely holding things together.
Q19: Having clear deadlines posted well in advance made things easier—I could plan around my chaotic schedule. What made it harder was when instructions were unclear or when I needed to interact with the LMS in specific ways at specific times. Also, the sheer volume of content across six courses was overwhelming. Simpler navigation and fewer clicks would have helped a lot.
Q20: I think that could be someone who's anxious and checking things repeatedly without actually being able to focus and complete work. Or maybe they're trying to understand the material but struggling so much that they can't produce anything. High activity doesn't mean productive activity—sometimes it's just spinning your wheels because you're stressed and can't move forward.
Q21: That's basically my story. My LMS activity was low, but I was studying offline, working through problems by hand, and really engaging with the material in ways that didn't show up in any log. I got decent grades because I learned the content, just not in the way the system could track. Sometimes less visible activity means more focused, deeper learning.
Q22: Course logs can show when someone logs in and what they click on, but they can't show understanding or effort or what's happening in someone's life. They miss all the offline studying, the mental processing, the conversations with classmates outside the platform. They also can't tell the difference between someone mindlessly scrolling and someone deeply focused for five minutes.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about and wrestling with the material, not just going through the motions. It's when I'm thinking about course concepts even when I'm not studying, making connections, asking questions in my head. For me, engagement happens in my hands and my mind—writing notes, working through problems—not necessarily in how often I click on a website.
Q24: The LMS can't show how hard I fought to stay in this course despite everything working against me. It can't show the anxiety attacks before assignments or the relief when I finally submitted something. It doesn't capture that I was working two jobs, dealing with family issues, and still managed to earn a Merit. My resilience and determination are completely invisible in those numbers.
